title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure items including a recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel for $126,900, a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and construction agreements for multiple road projects. The committee also discussed ongoing CIP projects, sewer discharge violations, and directed staff to implement $100/day fines for illegal sump pump discharges into the sanitary sewer system.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, a project previously scheduled for 2029 that will likely move to 2028. The $1.5 million project received over 50% grant funding. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Scofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and a tentative special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28th.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick, inlet repairs on Birch, and signing installation on Scofield Avenue. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment and servicing mowing equipment. Committee discussed flooding issues near Zenzer and Shore Avenue, with staff looking at permanent pump solutions.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system. In April, the village pumped 37 million gallons of water into the distribution system but treated nearly 54 million gallons at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified houses with sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers. The INI study with in-pipe technologies will help identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911, with 6% water loss. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with 6.7% infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Scofield/Rib Mountain. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the League of Wisconsin Municipalities' sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. Michael noted the village has decreased its public works budget in recent years and relies heavily on grant funding for one-time costs while needing recurring funding for maintenance and snow plowing. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across this year's three projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of illegal sump pump discharges into the sanitary sewer. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members distinguished between unknowing violations (warranting notification first) and willful violations (warranting maximum fines). After discussion, committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement allowing a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect to water and sewer mains via long lateral service until Radkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee requested adding a deadline, agreeing reconstruction should occur no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for construction on Military Road from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for utility work (sanitary sewer and water main relocation) is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA submitted $37,315 vs Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA submitted $70,400 vs Clark Dietz at $73,730. Committee approved MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue, preferring Clark Dietz's full-time experienced inspector over MSA's summer intern. Motion passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not to exceed $100/day
Authorize water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 Weston share)
Award Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services to MSA ($37,315)
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Consider adding street lighting to Scofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in 2030 project

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as secretary.
Minutes from April 21st were approved unanimously with a motion and second by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage from the Holiday Inn Express and Fairfield. The commission approved a $5,000 grant unanimously, matching last year's amount, with a request for detailed room usage data in their post-event report.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. The candidates visited Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, and this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide detailed hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. The commission also approved April 2026 minutes, certified survey maps, and building permits.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Jennifer from staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained challenges including a 36-inch gas pipeline requiring the building to be rotated and repositioned to the north to utilize the flat area and reduce fill costs. The building will have capacity for 50-60 officers (currently 40) with expansion possible to the north. An alternate storage shed was discussed with suggestion to place it further north to allow future building expansion. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve passed unanimously with addition that staff report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.
Motion to approve as presented was made and seconded. Passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Staff-approved items were presented. Motion to approve passed unanimously.
Motion to approve passed unanimously.

Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email and calendar invitations.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video conference. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the new police department to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on salt storage shed inspections, noting some zoning complaints are years old and get 'lost in the sauce.'
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with twice-annual inspections for facilities in wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee around May 2027
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that lacks conditional use permit
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Future agenda item: electric vehicle charging stations (expected fall 2026)
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts with commission

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an organizational meeting primarily focused on educating new board members about the committee's jurisdiction, departments, and responsibilities. Minutes from April 1st were approved unanimously. Department heads from Health, Veteran Services, and the Aging Disability Resource Center presented overviews of their operations, budgets, and key initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg participating remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties, and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the April 1st committee meeting minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. Motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Administrator Leonard explained the committee's structure under ordinance section 2.04 sub5, including oversight of Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and affiliated entities like North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He explained the committee receives input from the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, Board of Health, and others.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, and community health improvement teams. She highlighted public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, and a new homeless system response position. Supervisor Conway asked about vending machine locations - one at Community Partners Campus and one at Health First.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who could not attend. The department has nearly 140 FTE and a $22 million budget with about $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program conducted in partnership with the state, which addresses housing instability for child welfare and youth justice cases. Chair Boots noted this relates to a homelessness task force from the previous session that Supervisors Cavelli and Conway served on.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported his small department (3.0 FTE) had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. Veterans received $2.6 million in retroactive payments and $44 million annually flows into the county. Key initiatives include assisting surviving spouses with dependency and indemnity compensation benefits, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts - noting 30 suicides in Marathon County in 2025, seven of which were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about relationships with neighboring counties and staffing adequacy; Galindo confirmed good collaboration exists and noted every VSO office statewide is undermanned.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county regional agency serves as the one-stop shop for aging and disability services, including Meals on Wheels nutrition program with 385-400 volunteers. He noted significant financial challenges - federal funding flat since 2024 while nutrition costs rose 40-45% in four years, forcing meal and staffing reductions. The ADRC hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate nutrition program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare will be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray will present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Strategic plan discussion to be added to future agenda by Chair Boots
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the relocated farmers market at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of detailed cost data justifying the fees. Trustee Motti expressed support, noting the fees are justified as population increases demand more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 regarding remote meeting attendance. Discussion centered on removing specific justification requirements (health, safety, welfare) and making the policy broader, requiring only 12-hour advance notice, camera on, and prohibiting remote participation in closed sessions. An amendment to strike sentences requiring chairs to evaluate reasons for remote attendance was approved 5-2, with Trustees Hermling and Motti voting no. The amended policy then passed with the same 5-2 vote.

Chief Finkey explained the new reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Cloverleaf, and Riverside fire departments. Under this agreement, a food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker recognized by all, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressing the farmers market relocation from Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue corner (lost due to property development) to Kennedy Park. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass near the parking lot. Trustee Motti explained the previous location was donated by Ray Lockery for $1/year but is now being developed. Renee provided background noting the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots. The board discussed whether parking on grass damages the field and whether alternative locations exist. No action was taken; this was discussion only.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope Sorrell, approved unanimously. Parks, finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. The board agreed that future committee applications should be included in board packets.

Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street applied for secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying/selling precious metals with large dollar transactions requiring oversight similar to pawn brokers. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

Director Michael Dolan reported on the speed limit issue from prior meetings. The DOT engineering study showed 35 mph, but the board had directed 45 mph. A speed study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. The road is currently under construction so additional data collection is delayed. Trustee Pitel noted in public comment the speed is currently inconsistent with ordinance. Staff will bring back to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5202 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the village for hiring outside engineering consultants at $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before being officially appointed. He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park in the packet and called for greater accountability from the administrator.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective upon ordinance passage
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Mobile food vendor license ordinance adopted with reciprocal inspection agreements
E-bike regulations ordinance adopted
Nuisance ordinance amendments adopted
Resolution supporting sustainable transportation funding adopted
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspections of tornado-damaged homes
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Weston Avenue speed limit issue to return to board, possibly at joint meeting
Future committee applications to be included in board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following severe weather that devastated parts of the county, particularly the town of Ringle.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments presentation and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris
Vote on suspension of rules and tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspective, providing leadership development opportunities, and honoring supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion was carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion was carried but not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P [inaudible], seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion was carried but not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Dave Hagen Busher presented on the severe weather disaster affecting Ringle and other communities. The state declared emergency allows waiving the $13.10 state fee, Ringle is waiving their $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (from $65 standard rate) to cover costs. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely and extend the timeline beyond 60 days due to ongoing insurance delays. Supervisor Rosenberg, Supervisor Gao, Supervisor Leur, and others asked questions about policy implications and budgetary impacts. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested the county waive fees entirely for disaster victims, noting private businesses and the state have contributed but Marathon County has provided limited financial assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, requesting extended fee waivers due to insurance adjusters not yet arriving at many properties.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid waste management board directed to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Supervisors encouraged to attend Wisconsin Counties Association officials workshops

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that would only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Fee waiver consideration for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
Department overview - Parks, Recreation and Forestry (Tom Lovelin)
Department overview - Conservation Planning and Zoning (Lori Miskimmons)

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature and whether natural areas would be lost. Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Miskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individual tornado victims not in declared disaster areas. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to only waive septic system reconnect fees ($160), not other zoning or septic fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution then passed on a non-unanimous voice vote to forward to the county board.

Dave Hagenbusher presented Solid Waste overview covering landfill operations, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment requirements by 2032, and capital projects. Tom Lovelin presented Parks, Recreation and Forestry overview noting 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff, 17 county parks, and 30,700 acres of county forest. Lovelin announced his retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years. Lori Miskimmons began the Conservation Planning and Zoning presentation before the transcript ended.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only septic reconnect fees for tornado-impacted properties
Rezoning for Offer/Tract property forwarded to county board for approval
Rezoning for Schmidtz property forwarded to county board for approval
Staff to track costs associated with tornado-related permit requests
Tom Lovelin retiring June 1, 2026 - Joe Tucker selected as new county forest administrator

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved kayak launch signage for installation at three village locations, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course tree removal project. Public comment included opposition to dogs at the pool and criticism of Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. Motion carried with unanimous voice vote.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the project history from the comprehensive plan approval in November 2023 through the ribbon cutting on May 9th. Sean Oster explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently at approximately $85,000 toward a $117,000 goal. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds, with staff confirming no village dollars are currently earmarked as those items were pulled pending the Kennedy Park project. The Friends group plans to make a public donation announcement at their June tournament. The chair emphasized the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers and encouraged community involvement.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photographs and maps showing locations of fallen trees removed from the river and the installation of wooden mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats are now village property with extras available for future trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR Pipeline) was present during the work and conducted a walk-through afterward.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina, who had requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. Andy, a public commenter who spoke against allowing dogs at the pool, was informed his objection was noted and he was invited to return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: The chair raised concerns about graffiti and cleanliness on memorial benches and other amenities. Staff explained challenges with access - pressure washing equipment cannot reach the trail due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, sealer applications, and potential volunteer workdays. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Raasch from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version 7 of proposed signage for kayak launches along the Plover River. Three signs will be installed in the Village of Weston at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks launches. Signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and donation links to Marathon County Parks Foundation. The Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving a request from a community member. Signs would remind people to keep food at picnic tables rather than play structures. Committee agreed to bring this back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller park master plan process is beginning with Rettler, with a kickoff meeting scheduled for the following Tuesday.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy from 6504 [street inaudible] spoke in opposition to allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (appearing as 'Vern M' online) from 5002 [street] Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 matching receipts and expenditures 'too coincidental' and asking about the location of the $10,000 grant money and glow event proceeds.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate cleaning options and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail amenities
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at three Village of Weston locations
Food allergy awareness signage discussion to be added to future agenda
Mock Miller park master plan process to begin with Rettler kickoff meeting
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam about the clerk's office operations, election administration, licensing, and budget considerations. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about final numbers for TIF district closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October-November for bond payoff, noting the bonds are due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIFF reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of clerk's office operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee. Key points included: 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is done through walk-ins at clerk's office; they manage 51 liquor licenses; pet licensing was brought back in-house from third-party vendor Pet Data saving approximately $5,495 annually; and The Wire newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing. Discussion included poll worker wages being average for the county but chief inspector pay being on the lower end at current rate.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been present for 6-7 hours each Saturday since opening. Vendors are happy with the setup and location. Some Tuesday markets have had weather challenges. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and produce vendors with greenhouses. Committee members raised concerns about parking on both sides of Alderson and Newport streets causing traffic congestion.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. This was his last meeting. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and encouraged members to reach out to citizens who may want to participate.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Consider increasing chief inspector pay from $13 to $15 per hour at next election term
Continue seeking part-time clerk's office staff member
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots on Finance and HR Committee and other committees
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also approved fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors and discussed overnight parking regulations without taking action.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50 regarding nuisances, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (switched to 14 and 10 feet) and added language requiring compliance with jurisdiction authority. Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Committee discussed the model e-bike ordinance with significant focus on emotos (electric motorcycles/dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, and trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion addressed whether electric scooters should be exempt, with Chief Hunt suggesting adding 'with exemption to kids play scooters' for officer discretion. Olsen made a motion to recommend approval with the addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and any public property. Seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4 AM to 4 PM, October 31-May 1) is significantly longer than comparable municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Public Works Director Michael suggested eliminating the even/odd requirement and instead signing specific problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. No action was taken; the item was tabled for Michael to identify specific exception areas for the July meeting.

discussions.item: Crosswalk request on Alderson Street between Mount Olive Church and DC Everest
discussions.body: Staff presented a request for a crosswalk between Mount Olive Church and DC Everest High School. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for crosswalks requested by private entities. Chief Hunt noted no incidents in that area. Olsen made a motion that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost of installing the crosswalk, the committee recommends board approval. Seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive regarding cost sharing.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
discussions.body: Fire Chief explained a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Safer departments, where one inspection with a sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Chief noted a recent inspection where a food truck failed due to galvanized steel LP piping and improperly secured propane containers. Wausau opted not to participate, preferring their own system. Chwanka made a motion to approve ordinance 26-8 as written, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing corrections: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses 5915/5917), Ross Avenue properties requiring resequencing due to out-of-order numbering, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing even-numbered address. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski where an 8-plex has nine separate addresses including a utility room. Motion to recommend to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze order on an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue, abandoned since 2010-2011. A planned fire department training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated to be safe. The current owners had previously housed chickens inside the structure. Staff noted demolition costs will be placed on the property's tax roll. Olsen made the motion, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location at a park, predicting grass destruction by summer's end. He raised concerns about parking vehicles on grass potentially violating village code, ADA-compliant handicap spaces being placed on grass rather than asphalt, questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors, and noted ambiguity in food truck regulations regarding non-farmers market days. He also criticized the lack of accountability on long-standing code violations, stating some cases have been pending without visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for Board of Trustees approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on public property recommended for Board approval
Michael to identify specific areas needing parking exceptions for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost sharing
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections recommended for Board approval
Address corrections for E911 compliance recommended for Board approval
Staff authorized to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property conditions at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan

Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
